questions.question: Why are leaderboards more than a simple sorted-set problem in interviews?
options: Because update semantics, corrections, segmentation, and publication delays matter as much as ordering.
Because sorted data structures cannot be distributed.
Because users never ask for their own rank position.
Because anti-cheat concerns only affect logging systems.
questions.explanation: The surrounding semantics are what make the design senior-level.

questions.question: What usually makes around-me rank harder than top-N reads?
options: Top-N can be cached compactly, while around-me often needs deeper position lookup or approximation.
Top-N is impossible unless all writes are globally locked.
Around-me rank never depends on the leaderboard scope.
Top-N reads always require full leaderboard scans.
questions.explanation: The hot top of a ranking is often much cheaper to serve than arbitrary positions.

questions.question: Why might a leaderboard separate score acceptance from score publication?
options: Validation or anti-cheat checks may delay visibility for some updates without blocking everything else.
Publication is only needed for archived seasonal data.
Accepted scores are already guaranteed to be displayed in every scope immediately.
Separation removes the need to support corrections later.
questions.explanation: Separating these states lets the system stay responsive while still enforcing policy.

questions.question: Which signal best reveals that segmented leaderboards are causing a memory problem?
options: Per-scope set size, cache churn, and total active segment count rising faster than hot-read demand.
The number of users who view the help page for the game.
Average TLS certificate age across the company.
The count of leaderboard API endpoints in the source tree.
questions.explanation: Memory pressure shows up in segment footprint and churn, not random platform signals.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with approximate around-me rank?
options: It improves latency and cost at the price of less exact user positioning.
It guarantees better correctness than exact ranking.
It removes the need for tie-break rules entirely.
It makes score corrections impossible to apply.
questions.explanation: Approximation is a latency-cost trade, not a free win.

questions.question: Why should score corrections be part of the initial design?
options: Because fraud, moderation, and delayed verification can reorder rankings after initial acceptance.
Because leaderboards only accept decreasing scores in production.
Because sorted structures cannot support immutable events.
Because correction logic belongs only in analytics warehouses.
questions.explanation: Real systems need a plan for reversing or adjusting prior published state.

questions.question: What is the most credible mitigation for one player becoming a hot write key?
options: Aggregate or batch updates locally and add per-key throttles before ranking-state mutation.
Recompute the entire leaderboard after every score event.
Disable tie-breaks and publish only once per day.
Move all reads to the write leader database.
questions.explanation: Hot entities need bounded update work, not more global recomputation.

questions.question: Which redesign is most likely if regional leaderboards matter far more than global top-100?
options: Invest more in scoped materialization and local caches rather than optimizing only one global ranking.
Stop segmenting entirely and keep one worldwide sorted set.
Treat anti-cheat as optional for regional scopes.
Replace all score events with periodic CSV imports.
questions.explanation: The hottest product scopes should drive the storage and caching strategy.
